summary: O'Neil announces her project to build a decision tree that helps data practitioners choose the right algorithm based on the question they're trying to answer, not just technical factors like data size. She's using an online flowchart tool and has just started. She notes that existing algorithm cheat sheets focus too much on data characteristics rather than the underlying question being answered. The challenge is that mapping questions to algorithms is 'orthogonal' to how algorithms are typically taught, making the framework harder to construct.
key_points: Existing algorithm selection guides focus too heavily on data size and technical constraints rather than the conceptual question being answered
The mapping from 'what question am I asking?' to 'which algorithm answers it?' is underexplored and non-obvious
Many projects end up using clustering or classification even when the original question wasn't framed that way
Real student questions from her Lede Program would have been valuable examples of 'data questions in search of an algorithm'
money_quotes: And yes, I think it's kind of cool that 'decision tree' would be an outcome on my decision tree. Kind of like a nerd pun.

It was one of those moments when I said to myself, this morning at 6am, 'there should be a start-up that allows me to build a flowchart online!' and it totally worked. I almost feel like I willed gliffy.com into existence.
What kind of question are you answering with which algorithm?
Plenty of projects have ended up being clustering or classifying algorithms whose motivating questions did not originally take on the form 'how would we group these things together?'
The process of getting at algorithms from questions is somewhat orthogonal to the normal way algorithms are introduced.
key_insight: Choosing the right algorithm should start from the question you're trying to answer, not from the technical characteristics of your data—but this question-first approach is rarely how algorithms are taught.
